input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation relies on Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) practices.  At the architecture level SysML is employed to represent functional, logical and product/technical layers, with domain‑specific Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc and SysML I‑Blocks (IBDs) used to describe components, their interfaces, and inter‑connections.  Behavior is expressed with MontiArc Automaton or SysML v1.7 state machines, while a dedicated simulation and execution environment is provided by MathWorks Simulink, other simulation engines, and native general‑purpose code in C++ or Java.  Methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT guide the modeling hierarchy and abstraction.  These toolchains and frameworks collectively create the environment in which the Digital Twin is designed, simulated, verified, and deployed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models are assembled using Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) languages such as SysML, which supports functional, logical and product‑layer architecture via diagram types (e.g., IBDs). Behavioural models include SysML state machines and MontiArc Automaton. Physical models may be represented in geometric and analytical form and processed by simulation platforms such as MathWorks Simulink or other domain‑specific simulators. Simulation results and model data can be exported to code generated in languages such as C++ or Java for integration with control software. Data sources derive from the digital shadow, comprising one‑way automated streams of contextual data traces collected from the physical asset. Data management is performed within a hierarchical MBSE artefact repository that associates models across abstraction levels (requirements, functions, logic, physics) and links them to runtime assets through the SMArDT methodology.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: An automated, one‑way data flow transports time‑series numeric measurements from physical sensors—specifically position encoders and motor current—to the digital twin via MQTT. The data is continuously streamed and aggregated for further digital representation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The technical communication layer relies on MQTT to stream time‑series sensor data from the physical system to the digital representation. No additional topology, bandwidth, or security details are specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Digital twin engineering follows the same four‑step workflow as described for digital shadows, enriched with MBSE artefacts. Step 1 – Problem Identification defines the twin’s scope and validation criteria. Step 2 – Asset and Model Analysis constructs a component‑connector architecture using ADLs such as MontiArc or ACME and incorporates state‑machine or logic models. Step 3 – Calculations / Model Creation employs executable models (e.g., the MATLAB‑Simulink friction model) or analytical calculations and may involve physics‑based or statistical techniques. Step 4 – Data Identification captures the required telemetry and defines integration mechanisms. Quality assurance is achieved through validation of the simulation against benchmark data and sanity checks, with test and validation performed by comparing model outputs to real‑world measurements. Version control and evolution management are implied by the maintenance of MBSE artefacts and model code, but the document does not specify a particular VCS or evolution policy.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support is centered on an executable white‑box friction model implemented in MATLAB‑Simulink, which calculates the shaft’s first‑axis friction behaviour in real‑time. The twin presents these results through a dashboard that streams live simulation outputs, enabling operators to monitor anomalies or deviations. The twin does not deploy advanced analytics; instead, it relies on the fidelity of the white‑box model and the quality of the identified data streams, with confidence indicated implicitly by the simulation accuracy and the pre‑defined data‑quality thresholds.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin model adheres to Model‑Based Systems Engineering practices, employing SysML v1.7 for requirements, functional and logical architecture depiction and state‑machine modelling. It integrates with architecture description languages such as MontiArc and ACME, and executes simulations via MATLAB‑Simulink. The twin’s design conforms to the Digital Shadow reference model’s UML class diagram, ensuring alignment with established manufacturing modelling conventions.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction claims 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly identifies the physical system under study as the MABI MAX 100 industrial robot, specifically focusing on its first axis, motor, and gearbox components. Stating it is not in the document contradicts the evidence. Quotes: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'The first vertical robot axis at the base is used for creating a digital shadow', 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor'.

The extraction claims 'Not in Document', but the source documents describe specific services provided by the digital system (referred to as the digital shadow), such as real-time visualization in a dashboard and the continuous identification of joint friction to enhance motion accuracy. These are relevant digital services. Quotes: 'live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard', 'The resulting digital shadows enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction', 'enhance the robot’s motion accuracy'.

The extraction is highly detailed and accurately reflects the tools and methodologies described in the source text. It correctly captures the use of MBSE, SysML, specific ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc), simulation tools (Simulink), and modeling methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM). Quotes: 'ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML', 'describe the system’s components, their interfaces... ACME, AADL, MontiArc', 'combined with further (modeling) tools such as MathWorks Simulink'.

is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies

block_2_judge.reasoning: The documents mention 'live data', 'live measurements', and 'streamed via MQTT', indicating real-time data flow. However, they do not explicitly detail synchronization frequencies, specific latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs. The extraction states 'Not in Document', which is accurate as the provided text lacks these specific technical details regarding temporal aspects.

The text describes the creation of a single digital shadow for the 'first axis of the MABI robot'. There is no discussion of multiple digital twin instances, centralized versus decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between multiple twins. The extraction correctly states 'Not in Document'.

The paper presents a specific architecture for a 'digital shadow' (defined as passive) involving models (SysML, Simulink), data traces, and processing components for a robot. While it describes the orchestration of *this specific* shadow, it does not describe the broader 'orchestration and coordination of the Digital Twin system' in the general sense of system-wide patterns or resource management encompassing multiple distinct twins or active components. The text explicitly distinguishes the digital shadow from a Digital Twin. Thus, the broader 'dt_constellation' is not covered.

The text mentions 'live-data linking' and a 'dashboard' for visualization, as well as streaming via MQTT. However, it focuses on the internal loop of the robot and its shadow. There is no mention of interoperability standards, dependencies on external enterprise systems, or integration with other Digital Twins. The extraction correctly identifies that horizontal integration details are not in the document.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The source text mentions general background concepts like 'development phase' and 'operating time' but does not describe the specific lifecycle phases, transition management, or governance approaches of the Digital Twin as defined by the characteristic. The text focuses on the engineering methodology rather than the lifecycle stages themselves.

The extracted four-step workflow (Problem Identification, Asset and Model Analysis, Calculations/Model Creation, Data Identification) is accurately derived from Section II.C and IV.A. The use of specific tools like SysML, MontiArc, ACME, and MATLAB-Simulink is supported by Section II.A and IV.A. However, the specific claims regarding 'validation of the simulation against benchmark data and sanity checks' and 'evolution policy' are not explicitly found in the source text, which reduces the score slightly.

The description correctly identifies the use of an executable white-box friction model in MATLAB-Simulink and visualization via a dashboard (Sections IV.A and Abstract). However, it includes unsupported details such as 'pre-defined data-quality thresholds' and 'confidence indicated implicitly by simulation accuracy,' and paraphrases 'detection of friction loss' as 'monitor anomalies or deviations,' which adds unsupported nuance.

The extraction accurately lists the standards and tools used: SysML v1.7 for requirements and architecture, MontiArc and ACME as ADLs, MATLAB-Simulink for simulation, and conformance to the Digital Shadow Reference Model (UML). This is strongly supported by the Background section (II.A, II.B) and the Application section (IV).
